request.question: What was Alfed Dreyfus lawyer, Fernand Labori a victim of?

title: Fernand Lalonde
Hippolytus de Marsiliis
Fernand Choquette
Alfred Dreyfus
Fernand Dubé
Fernand de Brinon
Fernand Labori
Vera Brühne
Fernand Crémieux
Edgar Demange
sentences: Fernand Lalonde (born August 27, 1932) is a Canadian lawyer and politician.
Lalonde represented the riding of Marguerite-Bourgeoys in the National Assembly of Quebec from 1973 to 1984.
Hippolytus de Marsiliis (born 1451 Bologna; date of death unknown) was a lawyer and "doctor utriusque iuris" (Lat.
'doctor of either law' — one who studied civil as well as canon law).
He received his doctorate in 1480 but the date at which he became a lawyer is unknown.
Throughout his life, he wrote many repetitiones and notabilia on many canons and decretals.
In addition, he taught Roman law beginning in the year 1482.
He is best known for documenting the Chinese water torture method, in which drops of water would consistently fall on a victim's forehead, causing him to go insane.

He also was the first person to document sleep deprivation as a means of torture, wherein the interrogators would repeat same questions, shaking the victim at random intervals, pricking him with a sharp pin, or forcing him to march down a hallway endlessly.
If the interrogators grew weary, they would switch out with another group, who then would ask the same questions (today police use this method, but it is known as the third degree).
Fernand Choquette, {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} (October 27, 1895 – January 17, 1975) was a Canadian Quebec MNA and judge of the Quebec Court of Appeal from 1956 until 1970.
He also worked as a lawyer for over 10 years and a teacher for over 20 years.

Alfred Dreyfus (] ; 9 October 1859 – 12 July 1935) was a French Jewish artillery officer whose trial and conviction in 1894 on charges of treason became one of the most tense political dramas in modern French history with a wide echo in all Europe.
Known today as the Dreyfus affair, the incident eventually ended with Dreyfus's complete exoneration.
Fernand G. Dubé, QC (December 29, 1928 – October 5, 1999) was a Canadian lawyer and politician in the Province of New Brunswick.
Fernand de Brinon, Marquis de Brinon (] ; 26 August 1885 – 15 April 1947) was a French lawyer and journalist who was one of the architects of French collaboration with the Nazis during World War II.
He claimed to have had five private talks with Adolf Hitler between 1933 and 1937.
Fernand-Gustave-Gaston Labori (April 18, 1860 – March 14, 1917) was a French attorney.
He was born in Reims.
In his professional life, he defended the accused in some of the most prominent political cases of his day.
Among his noted clients was Alfred Dreyfus, who was eventually acquitted of treason.
During the Dreyfus trial, Labori was the victim of an assassination attempt which hospitalized him for a week.
The attacker was never identified.
Vera Brühne (February 6, 1910 in Essen – April 17, 2001 in Munich) became famous throughout Germany as a victim of miscarriage of justice.
In 1961/62, she was convicted, together with Johann Ferbach, of having murdered the physician Otto Praun and his lover.
Ulrich Sonnemann considered the case as a new Dreyfus affair.
In 1979, she was pardoned by Bavarian governor Franz Josef Strauss.
Fernand Crémieux (1857-1928) was a French lawyer and politician.
He served as a member of the National Assembly from 1885 to 1889, and from 1893 to 1898, representing Gard.
He also served as a member of the French Senate from 1903 to 1928, representing Gard.
Edgar Demange (April 22, 1841 in Versailles – February 1925 in Paris) was a French jurist.
He was, with Fernand Labori, the lawyer of Alfred Dreyfus during his trials in 1894 and 1899.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: What was Alfed Dreyfus lawyer, Fernand Labori a victim of?
hop1_docs: Fernand Labori | Fernand-Gustave-Gaston Labori (April 18, 1860 – March 14, 1917) was a French attorney.  He was born in Reims.  In his professional life, he defended the accused in some of the most prominent political cases of his day.  Among his noted clients was Alfred Dreyfus, who was eventually acquitted of treason.  During the Dreyfus trial, Labori was the victim of an assassination attempt which hospitalized him for a week.  The attacker was never identified.

Edgar Demange | Edgar Demange (April 22, 1841 in Versailles – February 1925 in Paris) was a French jurist.  He was, with Fernand Labori, the lawyer of Alfred Dreyfus during his trials in 1894 and 1899.

Mathieu Dreyfus | Mathieu Dreyfus (1857-1930) was an Alsatian Jewish industrialist and the older brother of Alfred Dreyfus, a French military officer falsely convicted of treason in what became known as the Dreyfus Affair.  Mathieu was one of his brother's most loyal supporters throughout the affair.

Ius Laboris | Ius Laboris is an international law firm network specializing in issues relating to employment law, employee benefits, and pensions.  As of 2015, the network had over 1,400 HR lawyers based in around 50 countries across Europe, the Americas, the Middle East, and Asia.
Camille Ferdinand Dreyfus | Ferdinand Dreyfus (5 May 1849 - 15 July 1915) was a wealthy French lawyer, historian and philanthropist who became Senator for Seine-et-Oise from 1909 to 1915.

Édouard Dreyfus Gonzalez | Édouard Vincent Joseph Dreyfus González de Orbegoso, 5th count of Premio Real (1876–1941) was a French Catholic businessman of Jewish descent, a lawyer, painter and writer, who was also an amateur composer under the pseudonym of Jean Dora.

Hubert Dreyfus's views on artificial intelligence | Hubert Dreyfus has been a critic of artificial intelligence research since the 1960s.  In a series of papers and books, including Alchemy and AI (1965), What Computers Can't Do (1972; 1979; 1992) and Mind over Machine (1986), he presented a pessimistic assessment of AI's progress and a critique of the philosophical foundations of the field.  Dreyfus' objections are discussed in most introductions to the philosophy of artificial intelligence, including , the standard AI textbook, and in , a survey of contemporary philosophy.
hop1_titles: Hubert Dreyfus's views on artificial intelligence

hop2_docs: Fernand Labori | Fernand-Gustave-Gaston Labori (April 18, 1860 – March 14, 1917) was a French attorney.  He was born in Reims.  In his professional life, he defended the accused in some of the most prominent political cases of his day.  Among his noted clients was Alfred Dreyfus, who was eventually acquitted of treason.  During the Dreyfus trial, Labori was the victim of an assassination attempt which hospitalized him for a week.  The attacker was never identified.

Edgar Demange | Edgar Demange (April 22, 1841 in Versailles – February 1925 in Paris) was a French jurist.  He was, with Fernand Labori, the lawyer of Alfred Dreyfus during his trials in 1894 and 1899.

June 1980 assassination attempt on Hafez al-Assad | On 26 June 1980, an assassination attempt on Hafez al-Assad, the Syrian president, was carried out by Muslim Brotherhood supporters who threw two grenades and fired machine gun bursts at him as he waited for an African diplomat in the Guest Palace in Damascus.  Assad kicked one grenade out of range, whilst one of Assad's bodyguards threw himself on the other grenade.

Assassinator Jing Ke | Assassinator Jing Ke is a 2004 Chinese television series based on a semi-fictional story of the assassin Jing Ke, who, in 227 BC, attempted to kill Ying Zheng, the king of Qin.  Directed by Raymond Lee, the series starred Liu Ye as the titular protagonist, with Peter Ho, Zheng Jiayu, Zhang Tielin, Shao Bing and Zhai Ying playing supporting roles.

Tony da Costa Fernandes | Tony da Costa Fernandes (born c. 1943) served as the Foreign Minister of UNITA, a rebel group in Angola.  In the 1990s Fernandes and UNITA Interior Minister General Miguel N'Zau Puna allegedly uncovered the fact that UNITA leader Jonas Savimbi ordered the assassinations of both Wilson dos Santos, UNITA's representative to Portugal, and Tito Chingunji, one of Costa Fernandes' predecessors.  Dos Santos and Chingunji's deaths and the defections of Fernandes and Puna weakened the U.S.-UNITA relationship and seriously harmed Savimbi's international reputation.

Assassination attempts on Hirohito | During the 1920s and 1930s, there were three known assassination attempts on Hirohito, the Emperor of Japan.  The assailants were all either Korean or Japanese.  Assassination attempts on Hirohito took place throughout his reign as Prince regent, and Emperor of Japan.  All of their attempts failed.  Of the four would-be assassins, two were sentenced to death, one was granted amnesty and eventually released, and one committed suicide before she could be tried.

Attempted assassination of Arthur Calwell | On 21 June 1966 an assassination attempt was made on Arthur Calwell then leader of the Australian Labour Party.  It was arguably the second assassination attempt in Australian political history following Henry James O'Farrell's attempt on the life of Prince Alfred.  (Although Percival Brookfield had been killed and Thomas Ley murdered several political opponents.)

response.summary_2: Fernand Labori was the victim of an assassination attempt. Fernand-Gustave-Gaston Labori was a French attorney who defended Alfred Dreyfus; during the Dreyfus trial the attempt hospitalized him for a week and the attacker was never identified.
